cards.text: A move constructor takes an {{c1::r-value reference}} parameter: {{c2::ClassName(ClassName&& other) noexcept}}
A move assignment operator signature: {{c1::ClassName&}} operator=({{c2::ClassName&&}} other) {{c3::noexcept}}

cards.front: When is a move constructor called instead of a copy constructor?
cards.back: When the object being constructed is initialized with an r-value (temporary object, std::move result, or object being returned by value).

cards.question: What should a basic move constructor do?
cards.answer: Steal the resource from other and nullify other's pointer.
cards.explanation:
Move constructor: data(other.data) { other.data = nullptr; }
This transfers ownership and leaves the source in a valid but empty state.

If you define any of these five special member functions, you should probably define all of them:
  • Destructor
  • Copy constructor
  • Copy assignment operator
  • Move constructor
  • Move assignment operator

cards.text: Move constructors and move assignment operators should be marked {{c1::noexcept}} because STL containers like std::vector will only use move operations if they're guaranteed {{c2::not to throw}}.

cards.question: What should you check at the beginning of a move assignment operator?
cards.explanation: Although rare with move assignment, self-assignment is possible with std::move, so it's good practice to check.

cards.front: Why are move operations typically faster than copy operations?
cards.back: Move operations transfer ownership of resources (like pointers) rather than duplicating the underlying data. This is typically just pointer assignment rather than allocating new memory and copying data.

cards.text: In a move assignment operator, you must {{c1::delete}} any existing resources before taking ownership of the new resources, just like in {{c2::copy assignment}}.

cards.question: When will the compiler generate a default move constructor?
cards.answer:
The compiler generates a default move constructor if:
  • No user-declared copy constructor/assignment
  • No user-declared move assignment
  • No user-declared destructor
  • All members are movable
cards.explanation: The compiler-generated move constructor will move each member.

cards.text: To prevent moving, explicitly delete the move operations: {{c1::ClassName(ClassName&&) = delete;}} and {{c2::ClassName& operator=(ClassName&&) = delete;}}

cards.concept: Valid but unspecified state after move
cards.explanation:
After an object has been moved from, it should be left in a valid but unspecified state:
  • Valid: safe to call destructor and assign to
  • Unspecified: don't rely on its value
  • Common practice: set pointers to nullptr, clear containers
examples: other.m_ptr = nullptr; // valid but empty state
